data.title: Japan woman mocked for ‘ugly’ looks, resists cosmetic surgery, transforms with make-up
data.description:
A 50-year-old Japanese woman, who was mocked for being “ugly” as a child, has shunned cosmetic surgery and spent 13 years taking more than 30,000 selfies to study make-up, skincare and fashion.
The ridiculing left Kozue Sakurada with deep emotional scars, but her persistence has inspired countless people online.
A male classmate once stared at her and repeated the word “ugly” to her face 10 times.

Other boys even made up a parody of a children’s song to mock her large nose.
By her early twenties, the trauma was so intense that if she saw someone covering their mouth to laugh on a train, she assumed they were laughing at her and would get off at the next stop in embarrassment.
The turning point came at the age of 37, after a heartbreaking break-up. Her then-boyfriend once looked at her and said: “If only you could be a bit prettier.”
Although she began trying to change her appearance to suit his preferences, about six months later, he still broke up with her.

It was then that Sakurada realised she wanted to become beautiful for herself.
Instead of turning to cosmetic surgery, she studied fashion trends through magazines and social media, and began taking daily full-body selfies in the mirror to analyse her appearance and seek improvement.
She experimented with different styles, adjusting skirt lengths by a centimetre at a time and trying various camisole necklines to see what best suited her body.
“It felt like conducting a scientific study,” she said.

To better understand what worked for her, she even underwent a “skeletal analysis” and concluded that her frame and facial features did not suit cute, girlie dresses.
Instead, casual styles like shirts and trousers suited her more.
However, at age 40, Sakurada suffered a breakdown during a psychology lecture.
Then, by revisiting her childhood hobby of sewing clothes, she finally began to heal.

“Even though I was changing my outfits, I still felt ugly deep inside,” she said.
“During a psychology workshop, I tried on a skirt I had made myself and felt it looked better than expected. I was genuinely happy. That moment made me realise that while other people’s opinions matter, what is most important is how I feel. I should wear what I want.”
Over 13 years, Sakurada took more than 30,000 selfies to study how to look more beautiful.
Eventually, she came to understand: “The key is not to lose yourself in the pursuit of other people’s approval.”
“I believe the true secret to beauty lies in the heart. The more we accept ourselves, the more beautiful we become. I still have room to grow on this journey of self-improvement, and I want to cherish that feeling and enjoy the process.”

Her story has inspired many people online.
One Japanese netizen wrote: “Rather than her physical features changing, I believe it is the result of her getting to know herself better. She changed in a beautiful way. Her beauty is the product of her own hard work: natural, not manufactured.”
A Chinese viewer said: “She is so beautiful now, it is like she glows. What is amazing is that she never took the short cut of plastic surgery. Truly admirable.”
A third person added: “This is incredible, not just a transformation in appearance, but a redemption of the soul. She pulled herself out of the swamp of self-denial.”

data.title: How the Osaka and Kyoto Ritz-Carltons blend classic luxury with Japanese tradition
data.description:
Whenever I visit a new destination, I am always in two minds when it comes to booking a hotel.
On the one hand, I want the familiar – the unapologetically indulgent trappings of luxury travel. In my experience, that means a classically Western style. Give me the marble and brass, mahogany and velvet. After a long day spent exploring new lands, I yearn to return to the comfort of a timeless, brand-scented lobby.
On the other hand, I want the property to belong to its address. I want the storytelling, the elevation of local artisans. I want – to use that deplorable travel cliché – an “authentic” experience. On a recent trip to vibrant Osaka and its quieter, historic neighbour Kyoto, I was able to enjoy both experiences over a long weekend courtesy of the Ritz-Carlton.

The Osaka property is located in the city’s Umeda district, a bustling business and shopping hub. Taking its cue from César Ritz’s late 19th‑ and early 20th‑century grand hotels in London and Paris, the hotel is opulent indeed. It opened in May 1997 as the first Ritz-Carlton hotel in Japan, and a 2024 renovation has modernised the amenities in the almost 300 rooms while maintaining the classic European style that is particularly popular with domestic travellers.
Contributing to the sense of timeless grandeur is around 450 works of art, most of which are oil paintings, and interiors in an 18th-century English style. The hotel’s restaurants are also true to the theme, with Michelin-starred French restaurant La Baie and Italian restaurant Splendido both leaning into classics and culinary tradition.

The hotel’s Japanese restaurant Hanagatami is organised into five areas, each with its own menu dedicated to a different facet of Japanese culinary culture: sushi, charcoal grill, kaiseki, teppanyaki and tempura. During my trip, the now-ended Flavors in Transit menu brought chefs from all five kitchens together to present a unique tasting menu inspired by railway station bento boxes. In the city known as the “kitchen of Japan”, Hanagatami offers guests the opportunity to tour the pantry without ever leaving the building.
The hotel’s Club Lounge also offers guests opportunities to explore Japanese cultural traditions, with daily Japanese tea ceremonies led by expert tea masters and interactive workshops teaching the Japanese art of furoshiki, wrapping gifts in cloth.

Neighbouring Kyoto is just 15 minutes away from Osaka by bullet train, but the smaller city couldn’t feel more different from the commercial energy of Osaka. Kyoto’s Ritz-Carlton property offers a similar departure in style and tone.
Opened in 2014, the hotel is situated on the bank of the serene Kamo River, overlooking the Higashiyama mountains. Its environs are reflected in the stunning entrance – an enveloping water feature that gently cascades down dozens of stone steps, culminating in a three-storey waterfall that ends at the hotel’s indoor pool and spa. The entrance sets the tone for everything within: a property inspired by its tranquil waterfront setting in the heart of Japan’s cultural capital.

Taking cues from a traditional ryokan, elements of Japanese design are abundantly evident in the hotel. Ribbed wood and grid patterns evoke traditional machiya town houses, while lacquer and rich embroidered silks are used throughout the property too.
The hotel’s collection of over 400 traditional and contemporary artworks – from delicate ceramics to dramatic textiles – features mostly Kyoto-based artists. Many works echo scenes, characters and garden settings from 11th-century novel The Tale of Genji, tying the interiors to Kyoto’s thousand-year literary heritage.

With light wood accents, muted textiles and floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking mountains, the guest rooms give an initial alpine chalet vibe. A closer look at every meticulously crafted detail, however, centres you firmly in the centuries-old city, inviting you to wrap yourself in one of the room’s traditional yukata robes and dive in. Take a soak in the deep tub, making use of the handmade soap from Japanese artisanal soapmaker Shabonya; brew a hot cup of hojicha, sourced from a Kyoto teahouse; settle into the cosy reading nook near the window – modelled after an engawa wooden veranda – and take in the view.
Dining options include Mizuki, which offers kaiseki, sushi, tempura and teppanyaki. Its lunchtime bento box of Kyoto-inspired obanzai small dishes presents an accessible, enjoyable way to sample the local flavours. In addition to the Lobby Lounge and Italian restaurant La Locanda, Chef’s Table by Katsuhito Inoue presents a kaiseki experience – a multi-course journey emphasising seasonal ingredients and artful presentation – inspired by Kyoto’s 72 micro-seasons.

Kyoto’s famous Gion geisha district is a 15-minute riverside walk away. The hotel also offers guided tours of some of Kyoto’s iconic spots. I spent my final morning on a dawn cycling tour to the Fushimi Inari-taisha shrine. Watching the sun rise over the Shinto shrine’s vermilion torii gates was an unforgettable end to a trip that offered both the comfort of polish and the thrill of place – a reminder that the best luxury doesn’t insulate you from a destination, it brings you closer to it.
The author stayed as a guest of The Ritz-Carlton Osaka and The Ritz-Carlton Kyoto.

data.title: Beijing looks for social workers to place on disputed South China Sea islands
data.description:
China is recruiting social workers for disputed islands in the South China Sea.
According to a notice published on Monday, the government of Sansha, the authority set up to administer the disputed territories, said it was looking for 11 “community workers” to post on eight reefs and islands in the Paracel and the Spratly islands.
The territories include Woody Island, the administrative centre of the disputed territories, and Mischief Reef, which is home to a large military base.
The notice was unclear about the nature of the roles, but it suggested they would play a role in day-to-day governance. Beijing recruited 16 people to similar positions in 2023.
It added that candidates should be aged between 18 and 35 years old, have a college degree and be “dedicated to island and community service”.
It said they should also be able to cope with long-term postings in “remote maritime locations”, which required exceptional physical and psychological adaptability.
The notice also said that applicants needed to “demonstrate strong organisational and interpersonal skills, relevant professional knowledge, a heart for public service, and a genuine commitment to grass-roots community work and long-term residence on the islands”.
It added that preference would be given to Communist Party members, veterans or their family members, or those with experience in maritime law enforcement, island-based community management or marine environmental protection.
Beijing has been expanding its presence in the South China Sea – where its claims overlap with those of Brunei, Malaysia, the Philippines and Vietnam – for more than a decade.
Satellite data shows that it has built new radar facilities, antenna fields and electronic warfare installations on Mischief, Subi and Fiery Cross reefs, according to the Asia Maritime Transparency Initiative.




China has also started dredging sand on Antelope Reef, according to Newsweek, prompting speculation that it may seek to expand its infrastructure there.
It has also been expanding its civilian presence, setting up facilities for fishermen on remote islands.
Beijing has also built schools, stadiums, shopping centres and cinemas on Woody Island, which has at least 400 civilian residents.
It also constructed facilities such as harbours and landing strips that could have a dual military-civilian use.
These projects have angered Vietnam, which has described them as “completely illegal and invalid”.

data.title: Tougher building checks may add costs, slow projects, Hong Kong lawmaker says
data.description:
Extra costs and the use of public resources may be incurred under the Hong Kong government’s proposed moves to increase professional scrutiny of building maintenance projects following the deadly Tai Po inferno, a lawmaker has said, calling for further discussion.
Election Committee lawmaker Andrew Lam Siu-lo, a town planner by training, said on Thursday that expanding the Urban Renewal Authority’s (URA) role in such projects might require extra time and money, urging the government and legislature to discuss the proposals’ implementation details in greater depth.
“We have reached consensus on strengthening supervision and providing more assistance to individual homeowners, but discussions are still lacking regarding the costs of the new measures,” he told a radio programme.
At the first meeting of the new Legislative Council term on Wednesday, government officials outlined a series of measures to reform regulatory mechanisms for building maintenance projects and crack down on bid-rigging.
Such measures were proposed in response to the deadly blaze in Wang Fuk Court of Tai Po in late November, which claimed at least 161 lives and displaced around 5,000 residents.
According to preliminary investigations, non-fire-retardant scaffolding mesh and polyfoam boards, applied to protect windows during renovation, were blamed for the rapid spread of the fire.
After the inferno, rumours of bid-rigging surfaced as the project budget ballooned from HK$152 million in September 2023 to HK$336 million last year when the owners’ corporation opted for the most expensive option.

Among the suggestions, the government sought to involve the URA, which mainly handles urban renewal and redevelopment projects, in the tendering and assessment processes for homeowners.
Property owners would also be required to hire consultants independent of contractors to oversee project progress, according to the proposals.
For buildings applying for government maintenance subsidies, property owners would have to accept contractors and consultants appointed by the URA.
For those which do not, the authority would still advise homeowners on which parties should win the bids.
Lam added that the government and legislature should also consider whether the URA had sufficient manpower and resources to scrutinise all maintenance projects, warning that the longer review process could delay urgent cases.
“The URA has already been saying they are running out of resources just conducting their current tasks and that they have to slow down,” he said.
“If we are really to involve URA in [tendering and assessment], I am expecting the government to grant it extra funds through the coming budget.”
Lam said more attention should be given to “three-nil” buildings, those without an owners’ corporation, residents’ organisation, or property management firm.
“What we discussed [on Wednesday] mainly focused on buildings with owners’ corporations. But for those without, maintenance projects can only proceed when all owners give unanimous consent, meaning such projects are rarely carried out,” he said.
“I think this is the biggest loophole and we should fill in the gap as soon as possible.”
Speaking on the same show, Federation of Trade Unions lawmaker Bill Tang Ka-piu expressed support for the proposed measure, saying it would help ease the burden on management committee members of owners’ corporations, who often lack professional engineering knowledge.
“After the Wang Fuk Court inferno, many owners’ corporations are at a loss as they are afraid of doing anything wrong … and many people choose not to participate in such affairs, leaving vacuum spaces for unscrupulous people and making residents distrust the system,” he said.
Tang conceded that the measures would bring extra costs to homeowners, but said residents should be willing to pay more for higher levels of safety.
But he noted that many residents had recently complained about some owners’ corporations rushing to push through major maintenance projects, apparently to avoid stricter regulations that would soon take effect.
“I hope enforcement agencies would identify such organisations that expedite the commencement of maintenance projects in the coming half a year and impose more preventive measures,” he said.
Separately, education sector lawmaker Tang Fei called for more mental health support for students, fearing that memories of the inferno could resurface when details of the investigation were widely reported in the coming months.
“We need professional but low-profile support for them,” he said, referring to the fact that such tasks should be largely left to professionals.
“But attention from society should be refrained. Do not remind them of [potential mental pressure] every now and then, and turn our attention into another form of pressure.”

data.title: Logistics giants SF Holding, J&T strike US$1 billion deal as Asian e-commerce booms
data.description:
Chinese logistics giants SF Holding and J&T Global Express announced a cross-shareholding agreement worth HK$8.3 billion (US$1.06 billion) on Thursday, as the Chinese companies accelerate overseas expansion amid growing cross-border e-commerce demand across Asia.
The deal will see SF Holding, Asia’s largest integrated logistics provider, acquire an 8.45 per cent stake in J&T for about HK$8.3 billion, while J&T will buy 4.29 per cent of SF Holding’s enlarged share capital for the same amount, according to filings with the Hong Kong stock exchange.
Upon completion, SF Holding will hold 10 per cent of J&T’s total issued share capital.
Hong Kong-listed SF Holding rose about 3 per cent to HK$36.4, while J&T Express climbed 1.87 per cent to HK$11.96 in early morning trading.
Under the share subscription agreement, SF Holding will subscribe 821.66 million newly issued J&T Class B shares at HK$10.10 per share. The subscription price represents a 13.97 per cent discount to J&T’s closing price of HK$11.74 on January 14, and equals the 90-day volume-weighted average price.

J&T will subscribe to 225.88 million newly issued SF shares at HK$36.74 per share, representing a 3.9 per cent premium to SF’s closing price of HK$35.36 on the same date. The SF subscription price also matches the 90-day volume-weighted average price.
The agreement would help SF Holding expand its international businesses, according to the Shenzhen-based company.
J&T said in a statement that “leveraging SF Holding Group’s core resources and mature operational capability in first-mile and cross-border line haul, [J&T] is well positioned to further expand the network coverage and enhance the competitiveness of its end-to-end cross-border logistics solutions”.
The mutual equity investment will also “help both sides expand service boundaries” in network resources, differentiated product offerings and customer bases, J&T said.
Founded in 2015 by Li Jie, a former Oppo Indonesia executive, J&T has grown into a global logistics service provider that operates globally, primarily in Southeast Asia, China, the Middle East and Latin America.
The agreement comes as both J&T and SF Holding expand their businesses through a flurry of acquisitions. SF Holding acquired a 51.8 per cent stake in billionaire Robert Kuok’s Kerry Logistics Network for HK$17.6 billion in 2021. Two years later, J&T signed a share transfer agreement with Shenzhen Fengwang Holdings, an SF Holding subsidiary, to acquire 100 per cent of Fengwang Express for 1.18 billion yuan.
Cross-border e-commerce between China and the rest of Asia is booming. In the first three quarters of 2025, China’s cross-border e-commerce imports and exports totalled about 2.06 trillion yuan (US$295.4 billion), up 6.4 per cent year-on-year, China’s state-run media People’s Daily said.
In Southeast Asia, Chinese e-commerce companies including Temu and AliExpress accounted for about 50 per cent of the local e-commerce markets, such as Indonesia, Thailand and the Philippines by 2024, according to Bain & Company.
AliExpress is operated by Alibaba Group Holding, which owns the Post.

data.title: Hong Kong’s Coleman Wong falls at final Australian Open hurdle, loses to Ymer in 3 sets
data.description:
Coleman Wong Chak-lam will have to wait a little longer to make the main draw of another grand slam after crashing out of Australian Open qualifying at the final hurdle on Thursday.
The Hongkonger was dumped out in three sets by Sweden’s Ellas Ymer, losing 7-6, 4-6, 6-3 in two hours and 15 minutes at Melbourne Park.
Still, making the third round of qualifying represented Wong’s best result in the senior competition for the year’s first major, having lost in the second round 12 months ago.
And the 21-year-old, who is 132nd in the world, is likely to wish he could take back a couple of moments in a close match at AZN Arena with an opponent ranked 43 places below him.
Three times in the open set Ymer clawed his way back when on the verge of going a break down, and at 2-2 in the tie break, the Swede reeled off four straight points to take full control. Wong battled back to 6-4, but could not save the set.
The pair held to serve to start the second, until Wong, who reached the third round of the US Open last August, finally broke Ymer in the seventh game.
Wong hit four consecutive winners to extend his lead before holding on to draw level, pushing the match to a decider.
There was little evidence that a medical timeout at the start of the third set had affected Wong’s performance, and he took his second break of the day immediately, only to give it right back with a series of unforced errors.
Ymer broke again to go 5-3 ahead and while Wong saved three match points in the next game, the Swede fired down an ace at deuce and then powered down a first serve that forced Wong to hit wide on his last shot.

data.title: Alibaba’s Qwen AI app hits 100 million users as upgrade broadens role in daily life
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding has rolled out a major update to its flagship Qwen artificial intelligence app, integrating it more deeply into the company’s expansive ecosystem, from online shopping to travel booking, as it pushes to make the service an AI-powered gateway to daily life.
The upgrade came as Qwen surpassed 100 million monthly active users (MAUs) as of January, said Wu Jia, vice-president of Alibaba, at a corporate event on Thursday in Hangzhou, capital of eastern Zhejiang province, where the e-commerce giant was headquartered. Alibaba owns the Post.
An AI assistant’s ability to “enter the physical world” was as important as its capacity to operate in the digital realm, said Wu, head of Alibaba’s Qwen consumer business group. “It should also help people get things done in everyday scenarios.”
The update builds on Qwen’s momentum since its public beta launch in mid-November, underscoring Alibaba’s efforts to fend off competition in China’s consumer AI market.

Qwen became the world’s fastest-growing AI app in November after recording the highest increase in MAU among AI products globally at 149 per cent during that period, according to data compiled by AI product popularity tracker Aicpb.com.
The app now coordinates seamlessly across Alibaba’s sprawling ecosystem built over decades, allowing users to complete tasks – from e-commerce and food delivery to ride-hailing, travel bookings and movie ticketing – with simple voice commands, saving users the hassle of navigating multiple services and endless clicks.
Wu cited as example planning a family getaway for the coming Lunar New Year – with Qwen automatically coordinating a wide range of services, including recommending destinations, comparing flight fares, booking hotels and suggesting attractions.
“Alibaba’s abundant supply [of services] and [insights into] large quantities of daily transactions across our whole ecosystem give us the competitive edge,” Wu said.
In his keynote speech, Wu showed using voice commands to ask Qwen to order 40 cups of milk tea from a nearby store and have it delivered to the event’s venue. The only time he needed to intervene was when he keyed in his payment code.
The update also included a “Task Assistant” addition that expanded the app’s digital tasks, with the company expecting AI to take over up to 70 per cent of such tasks within two years, according to Wu. Qwen could now handle more than what the company categorised as 400 digital tasks, from coding, office collaboration, data analysis and reporting to many others.
Alibaba was partnering with various professional database providers across sectors such as medicine, healthcare, finance, law and academics to ensure that Qwen produced accurate responses, Wu added.

data.title: Man shot by federal officer in Minneapolis as tensions flare over crackdown
data.description:
A federal officer shot a man in the leg on Wednesday night in Minneapolis, a week after the fatal shooting of a local woman by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent touched off angry protests and an intensifying clash between state leaders and the Trump administration.
The shooting followed a targeted traffic stop shortly before 7pm Central Time, when the man fled in his vehicle and crashed into a parked car, according to Tricia McLaughlin, a spokeswoman for the Department of Homeland Security.
He fled on foot, she added, but the officer caught up with him moments later and tried to make an arrest.
The encounter then escalated, according to McLaughlin. She said that as the officer struggled with the man on the ground, two other people emerged from a flat and joined the confrontation, striking the officer with a snow shovel and a broom handle. McLaughlin said the suspect then broke free and began swinging one of the objects as well.

“Fearing for his life and safety as he was being ambushed by three individuals, the officer fired defensive shots,” McLaughlin said. The man was struck in the leg.
All three retreated into the flat but were later taken into custody, she said. The man who was shot and the officer were both hospitalised. Their conditions were not immediately disclosed.
Around the time of the shooting, Minnesota Governor Tim Walz delivered an address in which he condemned the federal presence in his state. In unusually stark terms, he accused agents of pulling over people indiscriminately, including US citizens, and detaining residents at grocery stores, bus stops and schools. He described the operations as “a campaign of organised brutality against the people of Minnesota by our own federal government”.
The City of Minneapolis said it was “aware of reports of a shooting involving federal law enforcement in North Minneapolis” and urged the public to remain calm as authorities work to confirm additional details. In a series of social media posts, city officials demanded that Immigration and Customs Enforcement leave the city and state immediately and reiterated support for immigrant and refugee communities amid mounting tensions over federal immigration operations.
DHS said the man who was shot is a Venezuelan national who entered the US without authorisation. It was not immediately clear what charges he or the other two people might face.
The shooting unfolded against a backdrop of heightened tensions in Minneapolis, where thousands of federal agents have surged into the city as part of a sweeping immigration crackdown by the Trump administration.

The operation has drawn fierce criticism from local officials, civil-rights advocates and residents, who say federal agents are carrying out aggressive street-level enforcement with little transparency or oversight.
In an interview with Reuters published Wednesday, President Donald Trump said it was “sad to see on both sides” after the deadly shooting of Renee Nicole Good by an ICE agent in Minneapolis last week.
Trump was asked if the agent did the right thing. “I don’t get into right or wrong. I know that it was a tough situation to be in,” he told Reuters. “There was very little respect shown to the police, in this case, the ICE officers.”
On whether he would pardon the officer if he were charged and convicted in the shooting, Trump said: “I think that we’re gonna have to see what happens. That was a very unfortunate incident. We’re just going to see what happens”.
Federal agents began arriving in the Minneapolis–St Paul area in early December under what ICE called “Operation Metro Surge”.

data.title: 2 killed in Thai crane collapse just a day after another kills 32 train passengers
data.description:
A crane collapsed over a Thai highway outside Bangkok, crushing cars and killing two people on Thursday, just a day after another construction crane plunged onto a rail track in the country’s northeast, killing 32 passengers.
Thursday’s incident occurred just outside Bangkok in Samut Sakhon province, where a crane installed on an under-construction elevated highway toppled onto the busy road below.
Dashcam footage shared on social media shows the crane collapse onto unsuspecting drivers in a plume of dust and debris.
In the clip, drivers can be seen desperately reversing away from a billowing cloud blanketing stricken vehicles, while the toppled piece of machinery comes to rest on the road beneath.
There were “two dead with no injuries”, local police chief Sitthiporn Kasi said.

Thailand’s woeful construction safety record has shot back to the spotlight this week after back-to-back crane tragedies.
In Wednesday’s train accident in Nakhon Ratchasima province east of Bangkok, a crane toppled from a Thai-Chinese high-speed rail link onto a passenger locomotive passing below.
The impact sliced a carriage in half, sparked a fire and forced the train to derail, leaving 32 dead and dozens more injured, some critically.
Italian-Thai Development, the company responsible for the construction site, issued an apology late on Wednesday, promising to cover hospital costs and compensation for the bereaved families.
The accident zone is a crucial part of a US$5 billion China-backed link connecting Thailand to Laos and Kunming by high-speed rail by 2028.


Questions have been raised over the safety record of the Thai construction giant responsible for the train line and myriad other contracts on blue-chip infrastructure schemes as well as private and government buildings.
In March last year, a rare earthquake caused a multi-storey tower under construction to collapse in Bangkok, burying scores of people under a mass of concrete slabs, twisted steel and construction materials.
The director and employees of Italian-Thai Development were among 20 people charged over the collapse, which killed at least 90 people, most of them young migrant workers.
Five other people were killed last March in a crane collapse on a highway also linked to the construction giant.
Additional reporting by Agence France-Presse

data.title: 61 million passenger trips at Hong Kong airport in 2025, up 15% year on year
data.description:
Passenger trips at the Hong Kong International Airport reached 61 million in 2025, marking a 15 per cent year-on-year increase, after a full year of operation with the facility’s third runway.
The Airport Authority said on Thursday that the strong figures came on the back of a busy Christmas season, which saw over 200,000 daily passenger trips over eight days in December. Cargo throughput for the year also rose by 2.7 per cent to 5.07 million tonnes.
“The strong traffic performance in 2025 marks a new milestone, securing our position as the leading international aviation hub in Asia,” the authority’s CEO Vivian Cheung Kar-fay said, adding that the authority had won 80 awards last year.
“During the year, we also further strengthened connectivity by introducing 30 new destinations across the globe, including Abu Dhabi, Brussels and Dallas, while increasing flight frequencies on major routes,” she said.
Flight movements in 2025 rose by 8.7 per cent compared with the previous year, reaching 394,730, with the authority highlighting how the launch of the Terminal 2 coach hall and the “Park and Fly” service facilitated smoother transport for passengers in the Greater Bay Area.
According to the authority, in December alone, the airport handled 5.8 million passengers, a 13.5 per cent increase compared with the same period last year, while flight movements rose 7.1 per cent year on year to 35,920.
The rise in passenger volume came after the airport’s third runway system was launched in November 2024.
The authority said the number of passengers transiting through Hong Kong also recorded double-digit growth in December. While it did not reveal the actual figure, the authority noted that traffic to and from mainland China, Southeast Asia and North America recorded the biggest jumps.
The airport’s cargo volume also grew 3.6 per cent year on year to 462,000 tonnes in December. Exports and transshipments both increased during the month, with transshipments recording 18 per cent growth.
Cargo traffic to and from Southeast Asia, Europe and the mainland increased the most among key trading regions.

With the HK$141.5 billion (US$18.1 billion) third runway project, the airport’s passenger throughput capacity is expected to be raised in phases by 50 per cent to 120 million annually by around 2035, while the cargo volume will be doubled from its current 5 million tonnes to 10 million tonnes per year.
The boosted capacity is set to enhance the airport’s competitiveness in the region, particularly in the Greater Bay Area, Beijing’s plan to combine Hong Kong, Macau and nine cities in Guangdong province into a new economic engine.
Former lawmaker Gary Zhang Xinyu, a member of the Legislative Council’s transport affairs panel, told the Post previously that the term “hub” referred not just to Hong Kong as a point of arrival or departure, but to travellers’ preference for transiting through the city because of the airport’s efficiency.
